sections.title: Charter Reform
sections.body: During his monthly appearance at the Board of Supervisors, Mayor Lurie introduced a charter reform package. The three reforms include:
list: Moving city contracts to the control of the City Administrator. According to Mayor Lurie, this will cut red tape, save taxpayer dollars, and reduce delays.
Ballots will be made shorter and simpler by requiring a majority at the Board of Supervisors to add a measure to a ballot and remove the Mayor's ability to unilaterally place a measure on the ballot.
Increasing accountability and flexibility by allowing the Mayor to hire and fire department heads.
sections.body_after: The Bay Area Data Dump is working to analyze past elections to see how they informed Mayor Lurie's reform package and how many propositions would be infeasible under these reforms.
sections.prompt: What do you think of charter reforms? Do you have any inkling on whether you will support these reforms in the next election? Let us know!

sections.title: Permitting: Sidewalk Flower Stands
sections.body: In line with the Mayor's permitting initiative, he sponsored this item to modernize the sidewalk flower stand permit program. In the early 1900's, downtown San Francisco had 120 outdoor flower stands, today there are only 4. Under the current system, permits can only be passed down to another family member or transferred by the Board of Supervisors. Now, the Director of the Department of Public Works will have authority over these permits and vendors will be required to operate 35 hours a week and they can sell flowers and plants. Mayor Lurie hopes updating this program will reactivate flower stands downtown and create a clear path to small business ownership in the city.
sections.prompt: Have you seen new flower stands sprouting Downtown?

sections.title: The Women's Agenda
sections.body: In collaboration with District 7 Supervisor Myrna Melgar and Department on the Status of Women (DOSW), Mayor Lurie launched the Women's Agenda on March 19th. Through strategic planning, DOSW defined four core policy areas: Health, Safety, Economic Mobility & Security, and Civic Leadership & Community Engagement. DOSW has outlined goals and objectives for each of the four policy areas, each centered around improving the lives and wellbeing of women, girls, and non-binary people. By the end of 2026, DOSW will establish progress benchmarks and performance indicators and provide updates to the Board of Supervisors, Mayor, and Commission on the Status of Women.
sections.prompt: What do you think the results of the women's agenda will be? What goals and outcomes do you think will be the easiest/hardest to achieve? The Bay Area Data Dump will keep an eye on forthcoming reports, but in the meantime let us know what you think!

sections.title: 1939 Market Street – Affordable Housing Project
sections.body: The Mayor's Office of Housing and Community Development (MOHCD) accepted a $47 million grant for a 100% affordable housing project at 1939 Market Street. Mercy Housing/MOHCD will receive $35 million for housing construction and $12.5 million will go to city agencies for public transit and social programming. The project would include 185 affordable units, many of which are set aside for specific populations: 75 Units for Seniors, 55 Units for Veterans, 40 Units for Homeless Seniors, and 9 Units providing HIV+ Services. Supervisor Mandelman voiced strong support for this project, hoping it will lower the displacement rate of older LGBTQ+ residents in District 8. However, there is no pathway to legally make this housing for queer residents, but the non-profit Open House will create an 18-month plan for effective outreach to queer people and help them with the application process. Construction of 1939 Market Street will begin in February 2027 and end in February 2029 (35 months from approval by Mayor Lurie).

sections.title: Potrero Yard Modernization Project (2500 Mariposa Street; District 9)
sections.body: The Potrero Yard Modernization Project will update the existing infrastructure of the Potrero bus yard, which is over 100 years old. When the project was proposed, SFMTA said that the bus yard could hold 246 buses and accommodate 465 units of affordable housing. As of 2025, the SFMTA lowered the number of affordable housing units on the site to 100. According to SFMTA, the project would not be financially feasible if they had to pay for a reinforced podium, which would have cost $70 - $160 million to install, thus eliminating their ability to build housing on top of the facility. As for the proposed 100 affordable units, SFMTA stated the development depends on funding sources and market feasibility. Supervisors Chan, Dorsey, and Sherrill all expressed disappointment over the changes to the project and pushed for other solutions. Supervisor Chan stated to support the project she will need a written commitment from both the SFMTA and Mayor Lurie promising that funds for a reinforced podium and the 465 affordable housing units; Supervisor Sherrill also pushed for the reinforced podium to be built now so affordable housing can be built later. SFMTA said that the Supervisors' requests cannot be fulfilled without going back to the bidding process, which would cause the cost of the project to rise and become financially infeasible. If approved now by the Board of Supervisors and Mayor Lurie, the project will be completed by 2030 and cost ~$1.4 billion.

sections.title: The Commission Streamlining Task Force Recommendations
sections.body: Proposition E was passed by voters in 2024 and led to the creation of a commission streamlining task force, whose sole purpose was shrinking the size of the city's commissions. After a year of work, the task force released their final report in January 2025, calling for the elimination and consolidation of many commissions. Before the Board of Supervisors heard the recommendations and conclusions from the task force on March 17th, 2026, Supervisor Mandelman said it would be highly unlikely for any member to introduce the charter amendment to the Board to send to voters in November. Despite this, the task force presented their recommendation to the Board: Keep 86 active and effective bodies, Combine two bodies, Eliminate 36 inactive bodies, Remove 24 bodies from the city code, and Take no action on 4 bodies. During public comment, many speakers expressed outrage at the task forces' recommendations and compared it to the federal cuts made by DOGE. Douglas Hagman, a member of the real reform committee which placed Prop E on the 2024 ballot, was disappointed in the academic approach taken by the task force. "The charter is our constitution and cities around the country are looking at us to see if we are going to uphold our constitution or not," said Hagman in his closing remarks. Other speakers echoed Hagman, including a commissioner from the Immigrant's Rights Commission who stated that efficiency is important but not when it costs communities their voice and ability to be civically engaged. Public comment on this item continued for over two hours. Most recommendations made by the task force are unlikely to become anything other than recommendations.

links.text: Presentation given by the commission streamlining task force

sections.title: Hearing on the condition of Women in custody in local jails
sections.body: Last May, the Public Defender's office received a complaint about a mass strip search that took place at San Francisco's county jail 2. According to the women involved, sheriff's deputies made them strip in front of each other while the deputies laughed and continued recording with their body cameras. This incident, along with many other concerns, were brought under the microscope during a public hearing called by Supervisor Chen. The hearing was nearly four hours and included voices from the public and social justice organizations calling for accountability, while Sheriff Miyamoto was grilled by Supervisors about jail conditions. The Young Women's Freedom Center and TGI Justice Project spoke of the lack of sunlight and recreation at the jail, saying the only time individuals get light is when they are taken to the hospital. They spoke of psych services being delayed or ignored by the Sheriff's Department as an act of retaliation against individuals who speak up. They spoke of the trauma of strip searches and how they increase instability and recidivism, and called for them to be limited to individualized searches based on reasonable suspicion. After the presentation the women listed their demands of the Sheriff's Department and the Board of Supervisors. Then it was time for Sheriff Miyamoto to take questions, and take the time to respond to many of the claims made. He made it clear that the Sheriff's Department is working to get body scanners in the jail's housing units, since they would be a less invasive way to search for contraband. Strip searches will always be a part of the jail and is a key part of contraband control, according to Miyamoto, but he could not answer whether or not strip searches yield results. As for the Sheriff's Deputies involved in the May 2025 incident, Sheriff Miyamoto said it was not the deputies decision to perform a mass strip search, but their supervisors. Additionally, even if the Sheriff's Department terminated the deputies involved, as community members and organizations have requested, they could be reinstated through their unions' collective bargaining agreement, under a policy called binding arbitration. During the hour of questions, Sheriff Miyamoto frequently reiterated that his department does good work in San Francisco and is working towards being accountable and accessible to the public, community organizations, and the public defender's office.
